Process of Australian unification

For the implementation of Australia's federal system of government, see [Federalism in Australia](/wiki/Federalism_in_Australia "Federalism in Australia").

The **Federation of Australia** was the process by which the six separate [British](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire") [self-governing](/wiki/Self-governing_colony "Self-governing colony") [Australian colonies](/wiki/Australian_colonies "Australian colonies") — [New South Wales](/wiki/Colony_of_New_South_Wales "Colony of New South Wales"), [Queensland](/wiki/Colony_of_Queensland "Colony of Queensland"), [South Australia](/wiki/History_of_South_Australia#Proclamation_of_South_Australia_(1836) "History of South Australia") (which also governed what is now the [Northern Territory](/wiki/Northern_Territory "Northern Territory")), [Tasmania](/wiki/Colony_of_Tasmania "Colony of Tasmania"), [Victoria](/wiki/Colony_of_Victoria "Colony of Victoria"), and [Western Australia](/wiki/History_of_Western_Australia#Colonial_era "History of Western Australia") — united to form the [Commonwealth of Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia"), establishing a system of [federalism in Australia](/wiki/Federalism_in_Australia "Federalism in Australia"). The colonies of [Fiji](/wiki/Colony_of_Fiji "Colony of Fiji") and [New Zealand](/wiki/Colony_of_New_Zealand "Colony of New Zealand") were originally part of this process, but they decided not to join the federation.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

Following federation, the six colonies that united to form the Commonwealth of Australia as states kept the systems of government (and the bicameral legislatures) that they had developed as separate colonies, but they also agreed to have a federal government that was responsible for matters concerning the whole nation. When the [Constitution of Australia](/wiki/Constitution_of_Australia "Constitution of Australia") came into force, on 1 January 1901, the colonies collectively became states of the Commonwealth of Australia.

The efforts to bring about federation in the mid-19th century were dogged by the lack of popular support for the movement. A number of conventions were held during the 1890s to develop a constitution for the Commonwealth. Sir [Henry Parkes](/wiki/Henry_Parkes "Henry Parkes"), Premier of the Colony of New South Wales, was instrumental in this process. Sir [Edmund Barton](/wiki/Edmund_Barton "Edmund Barton"), second only to Parkes in the length of his commitment to the federation cause, was the caretaker [Prime Minister of Australia](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Australia "Prime Minister of Australia") at the inaugural [national election](/wiki/1901_Australian_federal_election "1901 Australian federal election") in March 1901. The election returned Barton as prime minister, though without a majority.

This period has lent its name to an architectural style prevalent in Australia at that time, known as [Federation architecture](/wiki/Federation_architecture "Federation architecture"), or Federation style.

## Early calls for federation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Federation_of_Australia&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early calls for federation")]

As early as 1842, an anonymous article in the *South Australian Magazine* called for a "Union of the Australasian Colonies into a Governor-Generalship."[[2]](#cite_note-2)

In September 1846, the New South Wales Colonial Secretary Sir [Edward Deas Thomson](/wiki/Edward_Deas_Thomson "Edward Deas Thomson") suggested federation in the [New South Wales Legislative Council](/wiki/New_South_Wales_Legislative_Council "New South Wales Legislative Council"). The [Governor of New South Wales](/wiki/Governor_of_New_South_Wales "Governor of New South Wales"), Sir [Charles Fitzroy](/wiki/Charles_Augustus_FitzRoy "Charles Augustus FitzRoy"), then wrote to the United Kingdom's Colonial Office suggesting a "superior functionary" with power to review the legislation of all the colonies. In 1853, FitzRoy was appointed as Governor of [Van Diemen's Land](/wiki/Van_Diemen%27s_Land "Van Diemen's Land"), South Australia and Victoria – a pre-federation [governor-general of Australia](/wiki/Governor-General_of_Australia "Governor-General of Australia"), with wide-ranging powers to intervene in inter-colonial disputes. This title was also extended to his immediate successor, [William Denison](/wiki/William_Denison "William Denison").[[3]](#cite_note-dab-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-adb-5)

In 1847, the [Secretary of State for the Colonies](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_for_the_Colonies "Secretary of State for the Colonies") [Earl Grey](/wiki/Henry_Grey,_3rd_Earl_Grey "Henry Grey, 3rd Earl Grey") drew up a plan for a "General Assembly" of the colonies. The idea was quietly dropped.[[6]](#cite_note-6) However, it prompted the statesman [William Charles Wentworth](/wiki/William_Wentworth "William Wentworth") to in 1848 propose the establishment of "a Congress from the various Colonial Legislatures" to legislate on "inter-colonial questions".[[7]](#cite_note-Tink2-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)

A few years later in 1853, a select committee formed by Wentworth to draft a new constitution for New South Wales endorsed a General Assembly of the Australian Colonies. This assembly was proposed to legislate on intercolonial matters, including tariffs, railways, lighthouses, penal settlements, gold and the mail. This was the first outline of the future Australian Commonwealth to be presented in an official colonial legislative report.[[7]](#cite_note-Tink2-7)

In 1857, Deas Thomson moved for a New South Wales Parliamentary Select Committee on the question of Australian federation.[[9]](#cite_note-9) The committee reported in favour of a federal assembly being established, but the government changed in the meantime, and the question was shelved.

Also in 1857, in Britain, Wentworth founded the "General Association for the Australian Colonies", the object of which was to obtain a federal assembly for the whole of Australia.[[10]](#cite_note-EB1911-10) While in London, he produced a draft bill proposing a [confederation](/wiki/Confederation "Confederation") of the Australian colonies, with each colony given equal representation in an intercolonial assembly, a proposal subsequently endorsed by his association. He further proposed a "permissive Act" be passed by [Parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Parliament of the United Kingdom") allowing the colonies of Australia or any subset of them which was not a penal settlement to federate at will. Wentworth, hoping to garner as broad support as possible, proposed a loose association of the colonies, which was criticised by [Robert Lowe](/wiki/Robert_Lowe "Robert Lowe"). The secretary of state thereafter opted not to introduce the bill, assessing that it would probably lead to "dissension and discontent", distributing it nonetheless to the colonies for their responses. While there was in-principle support for a union of the colonies, the matter was ultimately deferred while New South Wales Premier [Charles Cowper](/wiki/Charles_Cowper "Charles Cowper") and Henry Parkes preferred to focus on liberalising Wentworth's squatter-friendly constitution.[[7]](#cite_note-Tink2-7)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8d/StateLibQld_2_202947_Crowds_of_people_outside_the_Treasury_Building%2C_Queen_Street%2C_Brisbane%2C_Queensland%2C_1901.jpg/250px-StateLibQld_2_202947_Crowds_of_people_outside_the_Treasury_Building%2C_Queen_Street%2C_Brisbane%2C_Queensland%2C_1901.jpg)](/wiki/File:StateLibQld_2_202947_Crowds_of_people_outside_the_Treasury_Building,_Queen_Street,_Brisbane,_Queensland,_1901.jpg)

[Governor of Queensland](/wiki/Governor_of_Queensland "Governor of Queensland") [Lord Lamington](/wiki/Charles_Cochrane-Baillie,_2nd_Baron_Lamington "Charles Cochrane-Baillie, 2nd Baron Lamington") reading the Queen's proclamation on Federation in [Brisbane](/wiki/Brisbane "Brisbane")

### Federal Council of Australasia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Federation_of_Australia&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Federal Council of Australasia")]

A serious movement for Federation of the colonies arose in the late 1880s, a time when there was increasing [nationalism](/wiki/Nationalism "Nationalism") amongst Australians, the great majority of whom were born in the colonies. The idea of being Australian began to be celebrated in songs and poems. This was fostered by improvements in transport and communications, such as the establishment of a [telegraph](/wiki/Telegraph "Telegraph") system between the colonies in 1872. The Australian colonies were also influenced by other federations that had emerged around the world, particularly the United States and Canada.

Sir [Henry Parkes](/wiki/Henry_Parkes "Henry Parkes"), then colonial secretary of New South Wales, first proposed a Federal Council body in 1867.[[11]](#cite_note-11) After it was rejected by the British Secretary of State for the Colonies, the [Duke of Buckingham](/wiki/Richard_Temple-Nugent-Brydges-Chandos-Grenville,_3rd_Duke_of_Buckingham_and_Chandos "Richard Temple-Nugent-Brydges-Chandos-Grenville, 3rd Duke of Buckingham and Chandos"), Parkes brought up the issue again in 1880, this time as the [premier of New South Wales](/wiki/Premier_of_New_South_Wales "Premier of New South Wales"). At the conference, representatives from Victoria, New South Wales and South Australia considered a number of issues including federation, communication, Chinese immigration, vine diseases and uniform [tariff](/wiki/Tariff "Tariff") rates. The Federation had the potential to ensure that throughout the continent, trade and [interstate commerce](/wiki/Interstate_commerce "Interstate commerce") would be unaffected by [protectionism](/wiki/Protectionism "Protectionism") and measurement and transport would be standardised.

The final (and successful) push for a Federal Council came at an Intercolonial Convention in Sydney in November and December 1883. The trigger was the British rejection of Queensland's unilateral annexation of [New Guinea](/wiki/New_Guinea "New Guinea") and the British Government wish to see a federalised Australasia. The convention was called to debate the strategies needed to counter the activities of the [German](/wiki/German_Empire "German Empire") and [French](/wiki/French_Third_Republic "French Third Republic") in New Guinea and in [New Hebrides](/wiki/New_Hebrides "New Hebrides"). Sir [Samuel Griffith](/wiki/Samuel_Griffith "Samuel Griffith"), the [premier of Queensland](/wiki/Premier_of_Queensland "Premier of Queensland"), drafted a bill to constitute the Federal Council. The conference successfully petitioned the [Imperial Parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Parliament of the United Kingdom") to enact the *[Federal Council of Australasia Act 1885](/wiki/Federal_Council_of_Australasia_Act_1885 "Federal Council of Australasia Act 1885")*.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

As a result, a [Federal Council of Australasia](/wiki/Federal_Council_of_Australasia "Federal Council of Australasia") was formed, to represent the affairs of the colonies in their relations with the [South Pacific](/wiki/Australasia "Australasia") islands. New South Wales and New Zealand did not join. The self-governing colonies of Queensland, Tasmania and Victoria, as well as the [Crown Colonies](/wiki/Crown_colony "Crown colony") of Western Australia and [Fiji](/wiki/Fiji "Fiji"), became involved. South Australia was briefly a member between 1888 and 1890. The Federal Council had powers to legislate directly upon certain matters, and did so to effect the mutual recognition of naturalisations by colonies, to regulate labour standards in the employment of Pacific Island labour in fisheries, and to enable a legal suit to be [served](/wiki/Service_(law) "Service (law)") outside the colony in which it was issued, "a power valuable in matters ranging from absconding debtors to divorce proceedings".[[13]](#cite_note-13) But the Council did not have a permanent secretariat, executive powers, or any revenue of its own. Furthermore, the absence of the powerful colony of New South Wales weakened its representative value.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2d/Melbourne-Punch-federation-Victoria-pest-Australian-Chinese-May-1888.jpg/250px-Melbourne-Punch-federation-Victoria-pest-Australian-Chinese-May-1888.jpg)](/wiki/File:Melbourne-Punch-federation-Victoria-pest-Australian-Chinese-May-1888.jpg)

This 1888 [political cartoon](/wiki/Political_cartoon "Political cartoon") by the *[Melbourne Punch](/wiki/Melbourne_Punch "Melbourne Punch")* depicts the [anti-Chinese racism in Australia](/wiki/Anti-Chinese_sentiment#Australia "Anti-Chinese sentiment") which was one of the driving forces behind the push for federation.

Nevertheless, it was the first major form of inter-colonial co-operation. It provided an opportunity for [Federalists](/wiki/Federalist "Federalist") from around the country to meet and exchange ideas. The means by which the Council was established endorsed the continuing role that the Imperial Parliament would have in the development of Australia's constitutional structure. In terms of the *Federal Council of Australia Act*, the Australian drafters established a number of powers dealing with their common interests which would later be replicated in the Australian Constitution, especially [section 51](/wiki/Section_51_of_the_Australian_Constitution "Section 51 of the Australian Constitution").

### Early opposition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Federation_of_Australia&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Early opposition")]

The individual colonies, except Victoria, were somewhat wary of Federation. Politicians, particularly those from the smaller colonies, disliked the very idea of delegating power to a national government; they feared that any such government would inevitably be dominated by the more populous New South Wales and Victoria. Queensland, for its part, worried that the advent of [race-based](/wiki/White_Australia_Policy "White Australia Policy") national legislation would restrict the importing of [kanaka](/wiki/Kanaka_(Pacific_Island_worker) "Kanaka (Pacific Island worker)") labourers, thereby jeopardising its [sugar cane](/wiki/Sugar_cane "Sugar cane") industry.

These were not the only concerns of those resistant to federation. Smaller colonies also worried about the abolition of [tariffs](/wiki/Tariff "Tariff"), which would deprive them of a large proportion of their revenue, and leave their commerce at the mercy of the larger states. New South Wales, traditionally free-trade in its outlook, wanted to be satisfied that the federation's tariff policy would not be protectionist. Victorian Premier [James Service](/wiki/James_Service "James Service") described [fiscal union](/wiki/Fiscal_union "Fiscal union") as "the lion in the way" of federation.

A further fundamental issue was how to distribute the excess customs duties from the central government to the states. For the larger colonies, there was the possibility (which never became an actuality) that they could be required to subsidise the struggling economies of Tasmania, South Australia and Western Australia.

Even without the concerns, there was debate about the form of government that a federation would take. Experience of other federations was less than inspiring. In particular, the United States had experienced its traumatic [civil war](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War").

The nascent [Australian labour movement](/wiki/Australian_labour_movement "Australian labour movement") was less than wholly committed in its support for federation. On the one hand, nationalist sentiment was strong within the labour movement and there was much support for the idea of [White Australia](/wiki/White_Australia "White Australia"). On the other hand, labour representatives feared that federation would distract attention from the need for [social](/wiki/Reform_movement "Reform movement") and [industrial](/wiki/Labor_relations "Labor relations") reform, and further entrench the power of the conservative forces. The federal conventions included no representatives of organised labour. In fact, the proposed federal constitution was criticised by labour representatives as being too conservative. These representatives wanted to see a federal government with more power to legislate on issues such as wages and prices. They also regarded the proposed senate as much too powerful, with the capacity to block attempts at social and political reform, much as the colonial upper houses were quite openly doing at that time.

Religious factors played a small but not trivial part in disputes over whether federation was desirable or even possible. As a general rule, pro-federation leaders were Protestants, while Catholics' enthusiasm for federation was much weaker, not least because Parkes had been militantly anti-Catholic for decades (and because the labour movement was disproportionately Catholic in its membership).[[14]](#cite_note-14) For all that, many Irish could feel an attractive affinity between the cause of Home Rule in Ireland – effectively federalizing the United Kingdom – and the federation of the Australian colonies.[[15]](#cite_note-15) Federationists such as Edmund Barton, with the full support of his righthand man Richard O'Connor, were careful to maintain good relations with Irish opinion.

## Early constitutional conventions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Federation_of_Australia&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Early constitutional conventions")]

In the early 1890s, two meetings established the need for federation and set the framework for this to occur. An informal meeting attended by official representatives from the Australasian colonies was held in 1890. This led to the first [National Australasian Convention](/wiki/Constitutional_Convention_(Australia) "Constitutional Convention (Australia)"), meeting in Sydney in 1891. New Zealand was represented at both the conference and the Convention, although its delegates indicated that it would be unlikely to join the Federation at its foundation, but it would probably be interested in doing so at a later date.

### Australasian Federal Conference of 1890

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Federation_of_Australia&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Australasian Federal Conference of 1890")]

The Australasian Federal Conference of 1890 met at the instigation of Parkes.[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17) Accounts of its origin commonly commence with [Lord Carrington](/wiki/Charles_Wynn-Carington,_1st_Marquess_of_Lincolnshire "Charles Wynn-Carington, 1st Marquess of Lincolnshire"), the [Governor of New South Wales](/wiki/Governor_of_New_South_Wales "Governor of New South Wales"), goading the ageing Parkes at a luncheon on 15 June 1889. Parkes reportedly boasted that he "could confederate these colonies in twelve months". Carrington retorted, "Then why don't you do it? It would be a glorious finish to your life."[[18]](#cite_note-18) Parkes the next day wrote to the [Premier of Victoria](/wiki/Premier_of_Victoria "Premier of Victoria"), [Duncan Gillies](/wiki/Duncan_Gillies "Duncan Gillies"), offering to advance the cause of Federation. Gillies's response was predictably cool, given the reluctance of Parkes to bring [New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales") into the Federal Council. In October Parkes travelled north to Brisbane and met with Griffith and Sir [Thomas McIlwraith](/wiki/Thomas_McIlwraith "Thomas McIlwraith"). On the return journey, he stopped just south of the colonial border, and delivered the historic [Tenterfield Oration](/wiki/Tenterfield_Oration "Tenterfield Oration") on 24 October 1889 in [Tenterfield](/wiki/Tenterfield "Tenterfield")'s [School of Arts](/wiki/Tenterfield_School_of_Arts "Tenterfield School of Arts"), stating that the time had come for the colonies to consider Australian federation.

Through the latter part of 1889, the premiers and governors corresponded and agreed for an informal meeting to be called. The membership was: New South Wales, Parkes (Premier) and [William McMillan](/wiki/William_McMillan_(Australian_politician) "William McMillan (Australian politician)") (Colonial Treasurer); Victoria, Duncan Gillies (Premier) and [Alfred Deakin](/wiki/Alfred_Deakin "Alfred Deakin") (Chief Secretary); Queensland, Sir [Samuel Griffith](/wiki/Samuel_Griffith "Samuel Griffith") (Leader of the Opposition) and [John Murtagh Macrossan](/wiki/John_Murtagh_Macrossan "John Murtagh Macrossan") (Colonial Secretary); South Australia, Dr. [John Cockburn](/wiki/John_Cockburn_(Australian_politician) "John Cockburn (Australian politician)") (Premier) and [Thomas Playford](/wiki/Thomas_Playford_II "Thomas Playford II") (Leader of the Opposition); Tasmania, [Andrew Inglis Clark](/wiki/Andrew_Inglis_Clark "Andrew Inglis Clark") (Attorney-General) and [Stafford Bird](/wiki/Stafford_Bird "Stafford Bird") (Treasurer); Western Australia, Sir [James George Lee Steere](/wiki/James_George_Lee_Steere "James George Lee Steere") (Speaker); New Zealand, Captain [William Russell](/wiki/William_Russell_(New_Zealand_politician) "William Russell (New Zealand politician)") (Colonial Secretary) and Sir [John Hall](/wiki/John_Hall_(New_Zealand) "John Hall (New Zealand)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4c/Federal_Oak_1890.jpg/250px-Federal_Oak_1890.jpg)](/wiki/File:Federal_Oak_1890.jpg)

The Federal [Oak](/wiki/Oak_tree "Oak tree") in the gardens of the Victorian Parliament House in Melbourne. The tree was planted in 1890 by Sir Henry Parkes to commemorate the meeting of the Australian Federal Conference.

When the conference met at the Victorian Parliament in Melbourne on 6 February, the delegates were confronted with a scorching summer maximum temperature of 39.7 °C (103.5 °F) in the shade. The Conference debated whether or not the time was ripe to proceed with federation.

While some of the delegates agreed it was, the smaller states were not as enthusiastic. Thomas Playford from South Australia indicated the tariff question and lack of popular support as hurdles. Similarly, Sir James Lee Steere from Western Australia and the New Zealand delegates suggested there was little support for federation in their respective colonies.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/86/Australian_ogre_1900.jpg/250px-Australian_ogre_1900.jpg)](/wiki/File:Australian_ogre_1900.jpg)

Political cartoon from 1900 that shows the colonies of [New Zealand](/wiki/Colony_of_New_Zealand "Colony of New Zealand") and [Fiji](/wiki/Colony_of_Fiji "Colony of Fiji") rejecting the offer to join the Federation, with [Zealandia](/wiki/Zealandia_(personification) "Zealandia (personification)") referencing Australia's [origins as a penal colony](/wiki/Convicts_in_Australia "Convicts in Australia").

A basic question at this early assembly was how to combine federalism and responsible government. Parkes suggested the Canadian model, which federated with the *[British North America Act, 1867](/wiki/British_North_America_Act,_1867 "British North America Act, 1867")*, to be similarly adopted in Australia. However, delegates from the smaller states were not enthusiastic, with [John Alexander Cockburn](/wiki/John_Alexander_Cockburn "John Alexander Cockburn") of South Australia seeing the Canadian model as a "coercive" and "homogeneous National Union". [Andrew Inglis Clark](/wiki/Andrew_Inglis_Clark "Andrew Inglis Clark"), a long-time admirer of American federal institutions, introduced the [US Constitution](/wiki/US_Constitution "US Constitution") as an example of the protection of States' rights. He presented it as an alternative to the Canadian model, arguing that Canada was "an instance of amalgamation rather than Federation."[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20) A model closer to that of the United States was endorsed, with states able to act completely independently apart from those limited powers transferred to the federal government and where each state would be represented equally in a strong second chamber—the Senate.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

### Clark's draft constitution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Federation_of_Australia&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Clark's draft constitution")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/86/Andrew_Inglis_Clark.jpg/250px-Andrew_Inglis_Clark.jpg)](/wiki/File:Andrew_Inglis_Clark.jpg)

[Andrew Inglis Clark](/wiki/Andrew_Inglis_Clark "Andrew Inglis Clark"), circa 1907

Andrew Inglis Clark had given considerable thought towards a suitable constitution for Australia.[[22]](#cite_note-22) In May 1890, he travelled to London to conduct an appeal on behalf of the [Government of Tasmania](/wiki/Government_of_Tasmania "Government of Tasmania") before the [Privy Council](/wiki/Privy_Council "Privy Council"). During this trip, he began writing a draft constitution, taking the main provisions of the [British North America Act, 1867](/wiki/British_North_America_Act,_1867 "British North America Act, 1867") and its supplements up through 1890, the US Constitution, the [Federal Council of Australasia Act](/wiki/Federal_Council_of_Australasia_Act "Federal Council of Australasia Act"), and various Australian colonial constitutions. Clark returned from London by way of [Boston](/wiki/Boston "Boston"), Massachusetts, where he held discussions about his draft with [Oliver Wendell Holmes Jr.](/wiki/Oliver_Wendell_Holmes_Jr. "Oliver Wendell Holmes Jr."), and [Moncure Conway](/wiki/Moncure_Conway "Moncure Conway") among others.[[a]](#cite_note-23)

Clark's draft introduced the nomenclature and form which was subsequently adopted:

* The Australian Federation is described as the Commonwealth of Australia
* There are three separate and equal branches – the Parliament, the Executive, and the Judicature.
* The Legislature consists of a House of Representatives and a Senate
* It specified the [separation of powers](/wiki/Separation_of_powers_in_Australia "Separation of powers in Australia") and the [division of powers](/wiki/Federalism_in_Australia "Federalism in Australia") between the Federal and State governments.

Upon his return to Hobart in early November 1890, with the technical aid of [W. O. Wise](/w/index.php?title=W._O._Wise&action=edit&redlink=1 "W. O. Wise (page does not exist)"), the Tasmanian Parliamentary Draftsman, Clark completed the final form of the Draft Constitution and had a number of copies printed.[[23]](#cite_note-24) In February 1891, Clark circulated copies of his draft to Parkes, Barton and probably Playford as well.[[24]](#cite_note-neasey-25) This draft was always intended to be a private working document, and was never published.[[25]](#cite_note-26)

### The National Australasian Convention of 1891

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Federation_of_Australia&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: The National Australasian Convention of 1891")]

The [Parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_Australia "Parliament of Australia") proposed at the Convention of 1891 was to adopt the nomenclature of the [United States Congress](/wiki/United_States_Congress "United States Congress"); a House of Representatives and a Senate. The House of Representatives was to be elected by districts drawn up on the basis of their population, while in the Senate there was to be equal representation for each "province". This American model was mixed with the [Westminster system](/wiki/Westminster_system "Westminster system") by which the [Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Australia "Prime Minister of Australia") and other ministers would be appointed by the representative of the [British Crown](/wiki/British_Crown "British Crown") from among the members of the political party holding a majority in the lower House.

Griffith identified with great clarity at the Sydney Convention perhaps the greatest problem of all: how to structure the relationship between the lower and upper houses within the Federal Parliament. The main division of opinion centred on the contention of [Alfred Deakin](/wiki/Alfred_Deakin "Alfred Deakin"), that the lower house must be supreme, as opposed to the views of Barton, [John Cockburn](/wiki/John_Cockburn_(Australian_politician) "John Cockburn (Australian politician)") and others, that a strong Senate with co-ordinate powers was essential. Griffith himself recommended that the doctrine of responsible government should be left open, or substantially modified to accord with the Federal structure.

Over the Easter weekend in 1891, Griffith edited Clark's draft aboard the Queensland Government's steam yacht *[Lucinda](/wiki/Lucinda_(steam_yacht) "Lucinda (steam yacht)")*. (Clark was not present, as he was ill with influenza in Sydney). Griffith's draft Constitution was submitted to colonial parliaments but it lapsed in [New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales"), after which the other colonies were unwilling to proceed.

### Griffith or Clark?

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Federation_of_Australia&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Griffith or Clark?")]

The importance of the draft Constitution of 1891 was recognised by [John La Nauze](/wiki/John_La_Nauze "John La Nauze") when he flatly declared that "The draft of 1891 is the Constitution of 1900, not its father or grandfather."[[26]](#cite_note-27) In the twenty-first century, however, a lively debate has sprung up as to whether the principal credit for this draft belongs to Queensland's Sir [Samuel Griffith](/wiki/Samuel_Griffith "Samuel Griffith") or Tasmania's [Andrew Inglis Clark](/wiki/Andrew_Inglis_Clark "Andrew Inglis Clark"). The debate began with the publication of Peter Botsman's *The Great Constitutional Swindle: A Citizen's Guide to the Australian Constitution*[[27]](#cite_note-swindle-28) in 2000, and a biography of *Andrew Inglis Clark* by F.M. Neasey and L.J. Neasey published by the University of Tasmania Law Press in 2001.[[28]](#cite_note-29)

The traditional view attached almost sole responsibility for the 1891 draft to Griffith. Quick and Garran, for instance, state curtly that Griffith "had the chief hand in the actual drafting of the Bill".[[29]](#cite_note-30) Given that the authors of this highly respected work were themselves active members of the federal movement, it may be presumed that this view represents—if not the complete truth—then, at least, the consensus opinion among Australia's "founding fathers".

In his 1969 entry on "Clark, Andrew Inglis (1848–1907)" for the *Australian Dictionary of Biography*, Henry Reynolds offers a more nuanced view:[[30]](#cite_note-31)

> Before the National Australasian Convention in Sydney in 1891 [Clark] circulated his own draft constitution bill. This was practically a transcript of relevant provisions from the British North American Act, the United States Constitution and the Federal Council Act, arranged systematically, but it was to be of great use to the drafting committee at the convention. Parkes received it with reservations, suggesting that "the structure should be evolved bit by bit". George Higinbotham admitted the "acknowledged defects & disadvantages" of responsible government, but criticized Clark's plan to separate the executive and the legislature. Clark's draft also differed from the adopted constitution in his proposal for "a separate federal judiciary", with the new Supreme Court replacing the Privy Council as the highest court of appeal on all questions of law, which would be "a wholesome innovation upon the American system". He became a member of the Constitutional Committee and chairman of the Judiciary Committee. Although he took little part in the debates he assisted (Sir) Samuel Griffith, (Sir) Edmund Barton and Charles Cameron Kingston in revising Griffith's original draft of the adopted constitution on the Queensland government's steam yacht, Lucinda; though he was too ill to be present when the main work was done, his own draft had been the basis for most of Griffith's text.

Clark's supporters are quick to point out that 86 Sections (out of a total of 128) of the final Australian Constitution are recognisable in Clark's draft,[[27]](#cite_note-swindle-28) and that "only eight of Inglis Clark's ninety-six clauses failed to find their way into the final Australian Constitution";[[31]](#cite_note-32) but these are potentially misleading statistics. As Professor John Williams has pointed out:[[32]](#cite_note-:0-33)

> It is easy to point to the document and dismiss it as a mere "cut and paste" from known provisions. While there is some validity in such observations it does tend to overlook the fact that there are very few variations to be added once the basic structure is agreed. So for instance, there was always going to be parts dealing with the executive, the parliament and the judiciary in any Australian constitution. The fact that Inglis Clark modelled his on the American Constitution is no surprise once that basic decision was made. Issues of the respective legislative powers, the role of the states, the power of amendment and financial questions were the detail of the debate that the framers were about to address in 1891.

As to who was responsible for the actual detailed drafting, as distinct from the broad structure and framework of the 1891 draft, John Williams (for one) is in no doubt:[[32]](#cite_note-:0-33)

> In terms of style there can be little argument that Inglis Clark's Constitution is not as crisp or clean as Kingston's 1891 draft Constitution. This is not so much a reflection on Inglis Clark, but an acknowledgement of the talents of Charles Kingston and Sir Samuel Griffith as drafters. They were direct and economical with words. The same cannot always be said of Inglis Clark.

## Australasian Federal Convention of 1897–98

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Federation_of_Australia&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Australasian Federal Convention of 1897–98")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/30/On_the_box_seat_SLNSW_FL3725786.jpg/250px-On_the_box_seat_SLNSW_FL3725786.jpg)](/wiki/File:On_the_box_seat_SLNSW_FL3725786.jpg)

On the box seat, cartoon on Mr Reid and the federal premiership, 1899

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bb/Referendum_day_SLNSW_FL3725757.jpg/250px-Referendum_day_SLNSW_FL3725757.jpg)](/wiki/File:Referendum_day_SLNSW_FL3725757.jpg)

Handbill for Referendum Day (Tuesday, 20th June), and Friday 23rd June, 1899

The apparent enthusiasm of 1891 rapidly ebbed in the face of opposition from Henry Parkes' rival, George Reid, and the sudden advent of the Labor Party in New South Wales, which commonly dismissed federation as a "fad".[[33]](#cite_note-34) The subsequent revival of the federal movement owed much to the growth of federal leagues outside of capital cities, and, in Victoria, the [Australian Natives' Association](/wiki/Australian_Natives%27_Association "Australian Natives' Association"). The Border Federation League of Corowa held a conference in 1893 which was to prove of considerable significance, and a "People's Convention" in [Bathurst](/wiki/Bathurst,_New_South_Wales "Bathurst, New South Wales") in 1896 underlined the cautious conversion of George Reid to the federal cause. At the close of the Corowa Conference [John Quick](/wiki/John_Quick_(politician) "John Quick (politician)") had advanced a scheme of a popularly elected convention, tasked to prepare a constitution, which would then be put to a referendum in each colony. Winning the support of George Reid, premier of New South Wales from 1894, the Quick scheme was approved by all premiers in 1895. (Quick and [Robert Garran](/wiki/Robert_Garran "Robert Garran") later published *The Annotated Constitution of the Australian Commonwealth* in 1901, which is widely regarded as one of the most authoritative works on the Australian Constitution.[[34]](#cite_note-35)) In March 1897 took place the [Australasian Federal Convention Elections](/wiki/Australasian_Federal_Convention_Elections "Australasian Federal Convention Elections"), and several weeks later the delegates gathered for the Convention's first session in [Adelaide](/wiki/Adelaide "Adelaide"), later meeting in Sydney, and finally in Melbourne in March 1898. After the Adelaide meeting, the colonial parliaments took the opportunity to debate the emerging bill and to suggest changes. The basic principles of the 1891 draft constitution were adopted, modified by a consensus for more democracy in the constitutional structure. It was agreed that the Senate should be chosen, directly, by popular vote, rather than appointed by state governments.

On other matters there was considerable disagreement. State interests inevitably fractured the unity of delegates in matters involving rivers and railways, producing legalistic compromises. And they had few guides, at a conceptual level, to what they were doing. Deakin greatly praised [James Bryce](/wiki/James_Bryce,_1st_Viscount_Bryce "James Bryce, 1st Viscount Bryce")'s appreciation of American federalism, *The American Commonwealth*.[[35]](#cite_note-36) And Barton cited the analysis of federation of Bryce's Oxford colleagues, E.A. Freeman and A.V. Dicey.[[36]](#cite_note-37) But neither of these two writers could be said to be actual advocates of Federation. For delegates less given to reading (or citing) authors, the great model of plural governance would always be the British Empire,[[37]](#cite_note-38) which was not a federation.

The Australasian Federal Convention dissolved on 17 March 1898 having adopted a bill "To Constitute the Commonwealth of Australia."

## Federation referendums

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Federation_of_Australia&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Federation referendums")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/42/Australia_Federation_Ribbon2.jpg/250px-Australia_Federation_Ribbon2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Australia_Federation_Ribbon2.jpg)

A ribbon produced in Sydney

Referendums on the proposed constitution were held in four of the colonies in June 1898. There were majority votes in all four, however, the enabling legislation in New South Wales required the support of at least 80,000 voters for passage, equivalent to about half of enrolled voters, and this number was not reached.[[38]](#cite_note-ccwa-39) A meeting of the colonial premiers in early 1899 agreed to a number of amendments to make the constitution more acceptable to New South Wales. These included limiting the [Braddon Clause](/wiki/Edward_Braddon "Edward Braddon"), which guaranteed the states 75 percent of customs revenue, to just ten years of operation; requiring that the new federal capital would be located in New South Wales, but at least a hundred miles (160 km) distant from Sydney;[[38]](#cite_note-ccwa-39) and, in the circumstances of a double dissolution, reducing from six tenths to one half the requisite majority to legislate of a subsequent joint meeting of Senate and House. In June 1899, referendums on the revised constitution were held again in all the colonies except for [Western Australia](/wiki/Western_Australia "Western Australia"), where the vote was not held until the following year. The majority vote was yes in all the colonies.

## Federal Constitution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Federation_of_Australia&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Federal Constitution")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bf/Letters_Patent_Australia.jpg/250px-Letters_Patent_Australia.jpg)](/wiki/File:Letters_Patent_Australia.jpg)

The [letters patent](/wiki/Letters_patent "Letters patent") issued by Queen Victoria creating the office of [Governor-General](/wiki/Governor-General_of_Australia "Governor-General of Australia"), issued in 1900 as a part of the process of implementing the new federal constitution.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/48/Constitution_of_Australia.jpg/250px-Constitution_of_Australia.jpg)](/wiki/File:Constitution_of_Australia.jpg)

The [Constitution of Australia](/wiki/Constitution_of_Australia "Constitution of Australia")

Main article: [Constitution of Australia](/wiki/Constitution_of_Australia "Constitution of Australia")

The *Commonwealth of Australia Constitution Act 1900* (Imp) was passed on 5 July 1900[[39]](#cite_note-40) and given [royal assent](/wiki/Royal_Assent "Royal Assent") by [Queen Victoria](/wiki/Queen_Victoria "Queen Victoria") on 9 July 1900.[[40]](#cite_note-41) It was proclaimed on 1 January 1901 in [Centennial Park](/wiki/Centennial_Park,_New_South_Wales "Centennial Park, New South Wales"), Sydney. Sir Edmund Barton was sworn in as the interim [Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Australia "Prime Minister of Australia"), leading an interim Federal ministry of nine members.[[41]](#cite_note-:1-42)

The new constitution established a [bicameral](/wiki/Bicameral "Bicameral") Parliament, containing a [Senate](/wiki/Australian_Senate "Australian Senate") and a [House of Representatives](/wiki/Australian_House_of_Representatives "Australian House of Representatives"). The office of [governor-general](/wiki/Governor-General_of_Australia "Governor-General of Australia") was established as the Queen's representative; initially, as a representative of the British Government.[[41]](#cite_note-:1-42)

The Constitution also provided for the establishment of a [High Court](/wiki/High_Court_of_Australia "High Court of Australia"), and divided the powers of government between the states and the new Commonwealth government. The states retained their own parliaments, along with the majority of existing powers, but the federal government would be responsible for issues of defence, immigration, quarantine, customs, banking and coinage, among other powers.[[42]](#cite_note-43)[[43]](#cite_note-44)

## The economic consequences of federation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Federation_of_Australia&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: The economic consequences of federation")]

Australian federation entailed the creation of both a customs and a fiscal union. With respect to the customs union, tariffs were abolished on interstate trade (although this process occurred on a phased basis in Western Australia), while all of the colonies adopted the Commonwealth's common external tariff schedule in October 1901.[[44]](#cite_note-45) The first federal (Commonwealth) was widely regarded as protectionist; indeed, with respect to imports from outside of Australia, the average tariff increased relative to the average of the individual colonies' average tariffs prior to federation, according to estimates produced by Melbourne economist Peter Lloyd.[[45]](#cite_note-46) Nevertheless, the welfare-enhancing effect of the elimination of tariffs on interstate trade dominated the welfare-reducing effect of higher tariffs on overseas imports, such that the net static welfare gain from Australian federation was actually positive and estimated to have been 0.17% of GDP.[[46]](#cite_note-47) With respect to the fiscal union, there was a harmonisation of excise duties at approximately the mid-level of the colonial excise duties.[[47]](#cite_note-48)

## Landmarks named after Federation

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/43/Royal_exhibition_building_tulips_straight.jpg/250px-Royal_exhibition_building_tulips_straight.jpg)](/wiki/File:Royal_exhibition_building_tulips_straight.jpg)

The [Royal Exhibition Building](/wiki/Royal_Exhibition_Building "Royal Exhibition Building") in Melbourne is the site of the first sitting of Federal parliament.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8b/Citizens_arch_panorama.jpg/250px-Citizens_arch_panorama.jpg)](/wiki/File:Citizens_arch_panorama.jpg)

One of the many arches made to celebrate Federation, the Citizens Arch – National Museum, Canberra

The significance of Federation for Australia is such that a number of landmarks, natural and man-made, have been named after it. These include:

* [Federal Highway](/wiki/Federal_Highway_(Australia) "Federal Highway (Australia)"), between [Goulburn](/wiki/Goulburn,_New_South_Wales "Goulburn, New South Wales"), New South Wales and Canberra
* Federation Creek, near [Croydon](/wiki/Croydon,_Queensland "Croydon, Queensland"), Queensland
* [Federation Peak](/wiki/Federation_Peak "Federation Peak"), Tasmania
* Federation Range, on the Royston River, about 90 kilometres (56 mi) east-northeast of Melbourne, Victoria
* [Federation Square](/wiki/Federation_Square "Federation Square"), Melbourne, Victoria[[48]](#cite_note-49)
* [Federation Trail](/wiki/Federation_Trail "Federation Trail"), Melbourne, Victoria
* [Federation University](/wiki/Federation_University "Federation University"), Ballarat, Victoria
